name: Ama-no-Habakiri
description: Ama-no-Habakiri (天羽々斬) is a legendary sword in Japanese mythology, primarily known for its role in the myth of Susano-o-no-Mikoto slaying the Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. The name translates to "Heavenly Feather Cutter," suggesting its exceptional sharpness and ability to slice through anything with ease. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, Susano-o discovered the sword within the body of the Yamata no Orochi after slaying it. The sword was originally called Totsuka-no-Tsurugi (十拳剣, "Ten-Grip Sword") or Worochi-no-Aramasa. However, after Susano-o used it to cut the serpent into pieces and found that the blade was slightly notched, he renamed it Ama-no-Habakiri. Within the serpent's tail, he discovered the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, which he then presented to Amaterasu, his sister, as a peace offering. Ama-no-Habakiri is considered a sacred blade, embodying power, protection, and divine justice. Its association with Susano-o's heroic act cemented its place in Japanese folklore as a symbol of overcoming great evil and establishing order. The sword's legacy continues to be celebrated in various artistic and cultural expressions, representing the triumph of good over evil and the importance of courage and strength. While the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi is more widely known today, Ama-no-Habakiri holds a significant place in the sword's origin story and Susano-o's transformation from a mischievous god to a heroic figure.
subtitle: Heavenly Feather Cutter, Serpent Slayer
origin: Forged by the gods or discovered by Susano-o

keyMyths.title: Susano-o and the Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susano-o slays the eight-headed serpent, Yamata no Orochi, using the Ama-no-Habakiri. Within the serpent's tail, he discovers the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi.

keyMyths.title: Naming of the Sword
keyMyths.description: The sword was initially called Totsuka-no-Tsurugi, but after it was notched during the slaying of the serpent, Susano-o renamed it Ama-no-Habakiri.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another primary source for Japanese mythology.

worshipPractices.name: Sword Dedications
worshipPractices.description: While Ama-no-Habakiri is not directly worshipped, swords in general are often seen as sacred in Shinto. Dedicated spaces within shrines or homes might be used to honor swords representing power and protection, indirectly acknowledging the sword's significance.

worshipPractices.name: Susano-o Reverence
worshipPractices.description: Since Ama-no-Habakiri is inextricably linked to Susano-o-no-Mikoto, reverence for Susano-o implicitly acknowledges the sword's power. Prayers and offerings at shrines dedicated to Susano-o can be interpreted as an indirect acknowledgment of the sword's legendary status.

festivals.name: Gion Matsuri
festivals.description: The Gion Matsuri, particularly in Kyoto, celebrates Susano-o-no-Mikoto, the deity who wielded Ama-no-Habakiri. While the festival doesn't directly focus on the sword, the celebrations honor the hero who used it to defeat the Yamata no Orochi.

festivals.name: Autumn Harvest Festivals
festivals.description: Some autumn harvest festivals feature reenactments of the Yamata no Orochi slaying. These performances, while focused on the overall myth, serve as a reminder of the crucial role Ama-no-Habakiri played in securing prosperity and safety for the land.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ama-no-Habakiri continues to inspire artists and writers, often appearing in anime, manga, and video games as a powerful and legendary weapon. The sword symbolizes courage, strength, and the triumph of good over evil, themes that resonate strongly in modern storytelling and cultural expression.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Gram is a powerful sword used by Sigurd to slay the dragon Fafnir, similar to Ama-no-Habakiri's role in slaying Yamata no Orochi. Both swords are instrumental in defeating monstrous serpentine creatures.

Harpe, the sword used by Perseus to behead Medusa, shares the theme of a hero using a divine weapon to overcome a monstrous being. Both weapons are key to the hero's success against a seemingly insurmountable foe.

While not specifically used against a serpent, the Khopesh was a sickle-sword often depicted with pharaohs defeating enemies, embodying divine power and the ability to overcome chaos. Like Ama-no-Habakiri, it represents a tool for establishing order.

associations.name: Steel
associations.significance: Represents the sword's physical form and its inherent strength.

associations.name: Crimson
associations.significance: Symbolizes the blood spilled by the Yamata no Orochi, highlighting the sword's role as a slayer.

associations.name: Storm
associations.significance: Linked to Susano-o, who wielded the sword and is associated with storms and tempests.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: Embodies the act of slaying the monstrous Orochi, representing the triumph of good over evil.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Represents the Yamata no Orochi, the creature slain by the sword, making it a symbol of opposition.

associations.name: Sharpness
associations.significance: Reflects the sword's legendary ability to cut through anything, as indicated by its name 'Heavenly Feather Cutter'.

associations.name: Eight
associations.significance: Symbolizes the eight heads of the Yamata no Orochi, which the sword defeated.

companions.name: Susano-o-no-Mikoto
companions.description: Susano-o is the deity who wielded Ama-no-Habakiri to slay the Yamata no Orochi. He is the owner and primary user of the sword in the most famous legend.

companions.name: Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi
companions.description: Another legendary sword, Kusanagi was found within the Yamata no Orochi after Susano-o slew it with Ama-no-Habakiri. They are connected through the Orochi myth.

companions.name: Yamata no Orochi
companions.description: Although an antagonist, the Yamata no Orochi is intrinsically linked to Ama-no-Habakiri as the creature it was created to slay. The sword's legend is defined by this encounter.

companions.name: Inada-hime
companions.description: Inada-hime was the princess Susano-o rescued from the Yamata no Orochi, and she aided Susano-o by informing him of the creature's weakness. Her rescue is the reason Susano'o used Ama-no-Habakiri.

companions.name: Eight Vats of Sake
companions.description: The eight vats of sake used to intoxicate the Yamata no Orochi were essential to Susano-o's plan, enabling him to use Ama-no-Habakiri effectively. They acted as an important tool in the serpent's defeat.

extendedContent.title: Creation & Forging
extendedContent.content: While the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki detail Ama-no-Habakiri's discovery within the slain Yamata no Orochi, the actual forging or creation of the sword is shrouded in mystery. Unlike other divine weapons in Japanese mythology, such as Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi (later Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi) which has a clear lineage traced to Izanagi and Izanami, Ama-no-Habakiri's origin is not explicitly stated. This lack of a creation narrative fuels speculation. Some scholars suggest that its sudden appearance implies a divine origin, perhaps manifested directly from the heavens or crafted by an unnamed celestial smith. The absence of details regarding its forging could be interpreted as emphasizing the sword's inherent power, independent of any particular craftsmanship. Another theory posits that the sword was a pre-existing weapon of great power, simply rediscovered by Susano-o within the serpent's body, perhaps having been lost or hidden there by a previous owner. The act of finding it within the Orochi could then be seen as a symbolic act of Susano-o claiming his rightful place as a powerful deity. The ambiguity surrounding its creation adds to the mystique and aura surrounding Ama-no-Habakiri, reinforcing its status as a legendary artifact.

extendedContent.title: Powers & Properties
extendedContent.content: The name Ama-no-Habakiri, meaning “Heavenly Feather Cutter,” immediately suggests the sword’s defining property: unparalleled sharpness. It is described as being capable of slicing through anything, a feat demonstrated when Susano-o used it to dismember the Yamata no Orochi. Beyond its physical sharpness, the sword is imbued with a divine power, implied by its presence within the Orochi and its association with Susano-o, a powerful storm god. Some interpretations suggest that the sword possesses the ability to banish evil or purify corrupted entities, as evidenced by its role in defeating the monstrous serpent. The ability to cut through not just physical matter but also spiritual entities is a potential interpretation. Furthermore, the act of Susano-o finding Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi within the Orochi after using Ama-no-Habakiri to slay it suggests that Ama-no-Habakiri had the power to reveal hidden or dormant power, perhaps acting as a catalyst for the discovery of other significant artifacts. Although not explicitly stated, it is reasonable to infer that the sword also granted its wielder enhanced strength and combat prowess, enabling Susano-o to effectively wield such a powerful weapon against a formidable foe.

extendedContent.title: Notable Wielders
extendedContent.content: The primary, and arguably only, significant wielder of Ama-no-Habakiri is Susano-o-no-Mikoto, the Shinto god of storms and the sea. The Kojiki and Nihon Shoki unequivocally state that it was Susano-o who wielded the sword to slay the Yamata no Orochi. This act solidified Susano-o's heroic status and established Ama-no-Habakiri as a symbol of his divine power and skill. While there are no other documented wielders within the core mythological texts, the narrative surrounding Susano-o and the sword allows for extrapolation. It is conceivable that, within later interpretations or fictional adaptations of the myth, other figures might be depicted wielding a replica or a similar weapon inspired by Ama-no-Habakiri. However, these would be considered derivatives of the original myth. The sword is so closely tied to Susano-o that attributing it to another wielder would fundamentally alter the established narrative. The lack of other wielders emphasizes the unique bond between Susano-o and Ama-no-Habakiri, highlighting the sword as an extension of his own divine will and power.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism
extendedContent.content: Ama-no-Habakiri is rich in symbolic meaning, representing several key concepts within Japanese mythology and culture. First and foremost, it symbolizes the triumph of good over evil. The slaying of the Yamata no Orochi, a monstrous serpent representing chaos and destruction, with Ama-no-Habakiri embodies the victory of order and righteousness. The sword, therefore, becomes a symbol of divine justice and the power to overcome adversity. Furthermore, the sword represents the power of the storm god Susano-o. As a deity associated with both destructive storms and life-giving rain, Susano-o embodies a dualistic nature. Ama-no-Habakiri, as his weapon of choice in defeating the Orochi, reflects this duality, representing both the destructive force necessary to eradicate evil and the potential for renewal and creation that follows. The sword's ability to cut through anything can also be interpreted as a symbol of discernment and clarity, the ability to see through deception and illusion. Finally, the discovery of Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi within the slain Orochi after using Ama-no-Habakiri to defeat it can be seen as a symbol of revealing hidden potential or uncovering new power through righteous action.

shortDescription: Ama-no-Habakiri is a legendary Japanese sword used by Susano-o to slay the Yamata no Orochi. Its name, "Heavenly Feather Cutter," reflects its extraordinary sharpness.

wielders.name: Susano-o-no-Mikoto
wielders.context: Primary wielder; used to slay Yamata no Orochi

wielders.name: Toyotomi Hideyoshi
wielders.context: Claimed ownership in the late Sengoku period
